featuredSetups.label: Linked point view
featuredSetups.description: Open one reference point with the radius sweep, angle arc, and coordinate guides all visible.

setup.note: Use this when r, theta, x, and y should stay tied to one point.

featuredSetups.label: Second-quadrant bridge
featuredSetups.description: Start in Quadrant II and keep the same radius while the component signs change.

setup.note: This makes the sign change in x and y easier to read without losing the same radius.

pageIntro.definition: Keep one point visible in polar and Cartesian views at the same time so radius and angle turn directly into x and y on the plane.
pageIntro.keyTakeaway: A polar point is set by a distance r from the origin and an angle theta from the positive x-axis.
pageIntro.whyItMatters: Polar coordinates are the bridge between plane geometry, complex-number rotation, and later motion curves: one distance and one angle can locate the same point without abandoning x and y.

paragraphs: Polar coordinates become easier to trust when the same point stays visible in both polar and Cartesian language at once. This bench keeps r, theta, x, and y tied to one live point instead of turning coordinate conversion into a detached worksheet.

Changing radius should feel like sliding the point farther from the origin along the same direction. Changing theta should feel like sweeping the same point around the plane while x and y emerge from the same geometry.

keyIdeas: A polar point is set by a distance r from the origin and an angle theta from the positive x-axis.
The same point has Cartesian coordinates x = r cos theta and y = r sin theta.
Keeping radius fixed sweeps the point around a circle, while changing radius scales both coordinates together along one direction.

commonMisconception.myth: Polar coordinates and Cartesian coordinates are two separate descriptions, so converting between them is just algebraic bookkeeping.
correction: They are two views of the same point on the same plane.
The geometry explains the conversion: theta controls the direction, and r controls how far the point extends along that direction.

workedExamples.title: Worked example
workedExamples.intro: Use the reference-point preset so one fixed ray, guide set, and graph reading all stay aligned.

items.title: Read a reference Cartesian point from r and theta
items.prompt: For the reference-point preset, where does the point land in Cartesian coordinates?

steps.label: 2. Use the same geometry to resolve x and y
steps.template: The horizontal component is $x = r\cos\theta = 3.2(0.57) \approx 1.84$, and the vertical component is $y = r\sin\theta = 3.2(0.82) \approx 2.62$.

steps.label: 3. Write the Cartesian point
steps.template: So the same point lands at $(x, y) \approx (1.84, 2.62)$ in Quadrant I.

items.interpretationTemplate: The angle sets the quadrant and the component signs, while the radius scales both projections together along the same ray.

applyAction.label: Apply the linked point view
applyAction.observationHint: Keep the guides on so the radius ray and the Cartesian projections stay tied to the same point.

miniChallenge.prompt: Keep theta in Quadrant II, then make the point farther from the origin without changing the signs of x and y.
miniChallenge.prediction: Decide first whether you need to change the angle, the radius, or both.
miniChallenge.answer: You only need to increase the radius while keeping theta in Quadrant II.
miniChallenge.explanation: The quadrant and therefore the signs of x and y come from theta, while radius only scales how far the point sits from the origin along the same ray.

equations.label: Horizontal component from polar data
equations.meaning: The x-coordinate is the radius projected onto the horizontal axis.

equations.label: Vertical component from polar data
equations.meaning: The y-coordinate is the same radius projected onto the vertical axis.

equations.label: Recover polar information from x and y
equations.meaning: Distance from the origin gives r, and the direction of the point gives theta.
notes: The quadrant still matters when you interpret the inverse tangent.

variableLinks.label: Radius
variableLinks.description: Moves the point farther from or closer to the origin along the same direction.

variableLinks.label: Angle
variableLinks.description: Sweeps the point around the plane and changes the signs and sizes of x and y.

controls.label: Radius
controls.description: Controls how far the point sits from the origin.

controls.label: Angle
controls.description: Controls the direction of the point measured from the positive x-axis.

presets.label: Reference point
presets.description: A first-quadrant point with both components clearly visible.

presets.label: Second quadrant
presets.description: A longer ray in Quadrant II makes the sign change in x and y easy to read.

presets.label: Long shallow ray
presets.description: A larger radius with a small angle keeps x large while y stays positive but smaller.

overlays.label: Coordinate guides
overlays.shortDescription: Project the current point back to the x and y axes.
whatToNotice: The horizontal and vertical projections are exactly the x and y values of the same point.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the polar ray and the Cartesian coordinates tied to one geometry instead of two separate worksheets.

overlays.label: Angle arc
overlays.shortDescription: Show the swept angle from the positive x-axis to the current ray.
whatToNotice: The same theta that locates the point also determines which quadrant signs x and y should have.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps theta geometric instead of reducing it to a formula input box.

overlays.label: Radius sweep
overlays.shortDescription: Keep the full circle for the current radius visible while theta moves around it.
whatToNotice: Holding r fixed means the point stays on one circle while theta sweeps around it.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the theta sweep visible without turning the concept into a larger polar-graphs unit.

graphs.label: x and y vs angle
graphs.description: Sweep theta from 0 to 360 degrees at the current radius so x and y can be read as linked responses from the same geometry.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Keep the ray, the guides, and the coordinate sweep visible together.

items.text: Changing theta changes the signs of x and y because the ray crosses into different quadrants, not because the formulas became new formulas.
Increasing r stretches both x and y together along the same direction, so the point keeps its angle while moving farther from the origin.

predictionMode.title: Predict
predictionMode.intro: Predict the sign pattern before you apply the new polar state.

items.prompt: If theta moves into Quadrant II while the radius stays fixed, what should happen to x and y?
items.scenarioLabel: Move the point into Quadrant II

choices.label: x should be negative while y stays positive.
Both x and y should be negative.
x should stay positive while y becomes negative.

items.explanation: Quadrant II is left of the y-axis but above the x-axis, so the horizontal component is negative while the vertical component stays positive.
items.observationHint: Watch the same ray cross left of the y-axis while the coordinate guides stay on.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Use the radius ray, the coordinate guides, and the x-y sweep together. This checkpoint is about reading one point in both polar and Cartesian language without leaving the bench.

items.title: Radius-angle to x-y checkpoint
items.prompt: Build a point in Quadrant II where the leftward x projection is noticeably larger in magnitude than the upward y projection. Keep the coordinate guides on so $r$, $\theta$, $x$, and $y$ stay tied to the same point.
items.successMessage: You matched one polar point by reasoning through its Cartesian shadows instead of treating conversion as separate algebra. The ray and the axis projections now tell the same story.

setup.note: Use the live x and y readouts to check that the horizontal projection is more extreme than the vertical one.

hints: Quadrant II means $x$ must be negative while $y$ stays positive.
To make $|x|$ larger than $y$, keep the angle closer to $180^\circ$ than to $90^\circ$.

targets.label: Keep the radius near $3.7$ so the point stays on the longer second-quadrant ray.
Set the angle between $136^\circ$ and $144^\circ$ so the point stays in Quadrant II with a larger-magnitude x projection.

quickTest.title: Quick test
quickTest.intro: Answer from the live point, the guides, and the coordinate sweep.

questions.prompt: Which statement is correct for the current polar point?

choices.label: x comes from r cos theta and y comes from r sin theta for the same point.
x and y are separate from the polar point, so they must be solved on a different graph.
The angle sets x only, and the radius sets y only.

questions.explanation: The polar and Cartesian descriptions are two views of the same point, so both coordinates come from the same radius and angle.

showMeAction.label: Show the linked point view
showMeAction.observationHint: The coordinate guides show exactly where x and y come from on the same ray.

questions.prompt: If the radius increases while theta stays fixed, what should stay the same?

choices.label: The point should keep the same direction and quadrant.
The point should always move into a new quadrant.
The x-coordinate should stay identical while only y changes.

questions.explanation: Changing radius scales the point along the same ray, so theta and the quadrant stay fixed while the distance from the origin changes.

paragraphs: The simulation shows one Cartesian plane with a point, a radius ray, an optional angle arc, dashed coordinate guides to the axes, and readout cards that report both polar and Cartesian values for the same point.
One graph shows x and y changing together as theta sweeps from 0 to 360 degrees at the current radius.

seo.title: Polar Coordinates / Radius and Angle
seo.description: See one point in polar and Cartesian views at the same time, with live radius and angle controls that show how x and y emerge from the same geometry.

v2.intuition: Polar coordinates locate a point by keeping two jobs separate: r says how far from the origin, and θ says which direction the ray points.
v2.whyItMatters: Once you can read r, θ, x, and y as one linked picture, conversions stop feeling like memorized formulas and become reusable geometry for complex numbers, unit-circle trig, and parametric motion.

equationSnapshot.note: Use x = r cos θ and y = r sin θ as projections of the same radius ray; when going backward, keep the quadrant visible before trusting an inverse tangent angle.

guidedSteps.title: Read one linked point
guidedSteps.goal: Connect the polar readout and the Cartesian projections before changing anything.
guidedSteps.doThis: Start with the Reference point preset, keep the coordinate guides and angle arc visible, and compare the polar readout with the x-y readout.
guidedSteps.notice: The radius ray, angle arc, x-guide, and y-guide all point to the same dot on the same plane.
guidedSteps.explain: Polar and Cartesian coordinates are two descriptions of one point: r and θ locate the ray, while x and y are the horizontal and vertical shadows of that ray.

guidedSteps.title: Scale the radius
guidedSteps.goal: See r as a distance control that stretches the point along the same direction.
guidedSteps.doThis: Keep θ fixed, move only the Radius slider, and watch both projections grow or shrink together.
guidedSteps.notice: The quadrant does not change when only r changes; the point slides along the same ray and stays on the same angle.
guidedSteps.explain: Radius multiplies both projections, so x and y scale together while the direction and sign pattern stay fixed.

guidedSteps.title: Rotate through quadrants
guidedSteps.goal: Use θ to predict the signs of x and y before reading the formulas.
guidedSteps.doThis: Apply the Second quadrant preset or move θ into Quadrant II while the coordinate guides stay on.
guidedSteps.notice: The point moves left of the y-axis but remains above the x-axis, so x becomes negative while y stays positive.
guidedSteps.explain: The angle sets the quadrant first. The cosine and sine signs in the equations match the geometry of that quadrant.

guidedSteps.title: Check both languages
guidedSteps.goal: Explain one point using radius-angle language and x-y projection language together.
guidedSteps.doThis: Use the quick checks, then try the challenge: build a Quadrant II point where the leftward x projection is larger in magnitude than the upward y projection.
guidedSteps.notice: A good answer names both the quadrant sign pattern and the projection sizes, not just a slider value.
guidedSteps.explain: Polar reasoning is strongest when r, θ, x, and y stay tied to one visible point and each formula has a geometric job.

learned: A polar point uses r for distance from the origin and θ for direction from the positive x-axis.
x = r cos θ and y = r sin θ are projections of the same radius ray onto the coordinate axes.
Changing r scales the same direction, while changing θ rotates the ray and can change the signs of x and y.
When converting back from x and y, the quadrant must stay part of the angle decision.
wrapUp.misconception: Do not treat polar and Cartesian forms as separate points. They are two languages for the same point, and the live ray plus guides show why the formulas work.

nextConcepts.title: Inverse Trig / Angle from Ratio
nextConcepts.reasonLabel: Use quadrant-aware angle recovery when the direction has to be reconstructed from x and y.

nextConcepts.title: Parametric Curves / Motion from Equations
nextConcepts.reasonLabel: Carry the same linked x-y plane into motion where one parameter drives both coordinates.

wrapUp.freePlayPrompt: Pick a quadrant first, predict the signs of x and y, then move r and θ until the live point proves or corrects your prediction.
